shallow.synthGuide:
Write a tight sweep summary — first-pass knowledge capture, not a report. Aim for 300–400 words across all sections. Include what matters; cut the rest.

## Overview
What this topic is, where it stands now, and the key shift. 2–3 short paragraphs.

## Key Findings
3–5 thematic observations drawn from across the lanes. Be specific. Cite sources inline.

## Open Questions
What remains unsettled or unresolved. 3–5 items.

standard.synthGuide:
Write a substantive sweep summary. Aim for 700–900 words. Go beyond surface coverage — synthesise across lanes, note where perspectives diverge, and surface specific evidence. Name papers, people, organisations, and numbers.

## Overview
What this topic is, where it stands now, and what the key shift has been. 3 paragraphs.

## Timeline
A Mermaid `timeline` diagram of the key conceptual milestones in how this topic developed — inflection points, capability shifts, regulatory moves, market events, and the arrival of important new ideas — not a catalogue of individual papers or posts. Each entry should name the development itself (for example "prompt caching becomes a standard provider feature" or "memory staleness recognised as a measurable problem"), not cite a single source (for example "EPIC caching paper"). Where several sources point to the same shift, collapse them into one entry. Place this near the top so the reader gets the chronology before the analysis. Include only developments you can actually date. Pick the granularity from the timeline's total span: if it covers three years or fewer, use quarters (Q1 = Jan–Mar, Q2 = Apr–Jun, Q3 = Jul–Sep, Q4 = Oct–Dec); if it covers more than three years, use half-years (H1 = Jan–Jun, H2 = Jul–Dec); if it spans ten years or more, use whole years. Do NOT use `section`; make each period its own line with the year folded into the label (for example `H1 2023`, `Q3 2025`, or just `2018` at year granularity). Order chronologically and keep each entry to a short phrase. When a period has more than one event, list them all on a single colon-separated line (`H1 2023 : first event : second event`) so they render as several boxes under one period header. Use exactly this fenced format:

```mermaid
timeline
    title Key milestones, <date range>
    H1 2024 : A first milestone
    H2 2024 : Another milestone : A second in this period
    H1 2025 : A 2025 milestone : Another 2025 milestone
```

Omit this section only if the sweep surfaced fewer than three datable events.

## Key Findings
5–7 thematic observations drawn from across all lanes. Each should name a specific source, number, or example. Cite inline.

## Evidence & Data
The most important quantitative findings, benchmarks, adoption figures, or empirical results from the sweep. Name the source for every number.

## Tensions & Open Questions
Where the evidence is contested, incomplete, or evolving. 4–5 items.

deep.synthGuide:
Write a thorough research synthesis. Aim for 1800–2400 words. This is a deep sweep with high source coverage — the summary must reflect that depth through sustained analytical prose, not lists. Each section should develop its argument across multiple paragraphs. Synthesise across all lanes, surface specific data points and named evidence, and identify where lanes converge or contradict. A reader should finish with a detailed working knowledge of the topic sufficient to write about it authoritatively.

## Overview
Establish the topic: what it is, why it matters now, and what the defining shift of the past 18 months has been. 3–4 paragraphs. Do not hedge.

## Timeline
A Mermaid `timeline` diagram of the key conceptual milestones in how this topic developed — inflection points, capability shifts, regulatory moves, market events, and the arrival of important new ideas — not a catalogue of individual papers or posts. Each entry should name the development itself (for example "prompt caching becomes a standard provider feature" or "memory staleness recognised as a measurable problem"), not cite a single source (for example "EPIC caching paper"). Where several sources point to the same shift, collapse them into one entry. Place this near the top so the reader gets the chronology before the analysis. Include only developments you can actually date. Pick the granularity from the timeline's total span: if it covers three years or fewer, use quarters (Q1 = Jan–Mar, Q2 = Apr–Jun, Q3 = Jul–Sep, Q4 = Oct–Dec); if it covers more than three years, use half-years (H1 = Jan–Jun, H2 = Jul–Dec); if it spans ten years or more, use whole years. Do NOT use `section`; make each period its own line with the year folded into the label (for example `H1 2023`, `Q3 2025`, or just `2018` at year granularity). Order chronologically and keep each entry to a short phrase. When a period has more than one event, list them all on a single colon-separated line (`H1 2023 : first event : second event`) so they render as several boxes under one period header. Use exactly this fenced format:

```mermaid
timeline
    title Key milestones, <date range>
    H1 2024 : A first milestone
    H2 2024 : Another milestone : A second in this period
    H1 2025 : A 2025 milestone : Another 2025 milestone
```

Omit this section only if the sweep surfaced fewer than three datable events.

## Key Findings
7–10 substantive thematic observations. Each must be grounded in specific evidence from the sources — name the study, report, organisation, or person. Cite inline. Prioritise findings that cross lane boundaries (e.g. academic evidence that confirms or contradicts a VC thesis).

## Evidence & Data
A focused section on the most important quantitative findings, benchmarks, adoption figures, or empirical results surfaced in the sweep. Name the source for every number.

## Signals & Tensions
Where the picture is contested, evolving, or where lanes disagree. What is the weak signal that is not yet consensus? What is overhyped vs underreported? 4–6 items.

## Open Questions
What remains genuinely unresolved — not rhetorical questions, but gaps in the evidence or live debates. 5–7 items.
